basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Muṭaffifīn — “The Defrauders” — opens on a precise moral denunciation: woe to those who demand full measure when others serve them, and skimp when it is their turn to serve. But Qushayrī widens the scope of the word at once: defrauding is not only in weighing — it is in all the inequalities one grants oneself without granting them to others: he who sees others' faults without seeing his own, he who claims his right without rendering rights, he who does not want for his brother what he wants for himself. And he sets against these men a figure: al-fatā (الفَتَى, the noble youth) — “he who renders the rights of people and claims his own right from no one.” The sūra then rises toward two symmetrical places: Sijjīn (سِجِّين), where the deeds of the libertines are recorded, and ʿIlliyyīn (عِلِّيِّين), the Heights where those of the just are recorded. At the heart of the sūra, an overwhelming verse: they shall, that Day, be veiled from their Lord. Qushayrī draws from it a celebrated theological inference: “As they are today prevented from His knowledge, they are tomorrow prevented from His sight. The logic of the verse entails that the believers, for their part, will see Him tomorrow as they know Him today.” The worst of punishments, in this sūra, is not the Fire — it is the veil.

versets.trad: Woe to the defrauders!
versets.commentaire: wayl (وَيْل) — a word of imprecation uttered at the arrival of calamity. al-muṭaffif (المُطَفِّف): one who diminishes measure and weight. Qushayrī widens its scope at once: “He means by it those who, in their dealings with people, take full measure for themselves and give back a diminished one. This shows in weighing and measuring, but also in pointing out defects [in others while not seeing one's own], in judgment, in discharge of debts and in claims.”

mots.def: wayl: woe, imprecation uttered before calamity.
muṭaffif: one who diminishes measure or weight. Root ṭ-f-f, naming the little, what scarcely overflows the brim.
mots.gram: preposition + plural active participle, definite genitive

versets.ar: الَّذِينَ إِذَا اكْتَالُوا عَلَى النَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ
versets.tr: alladhīna idhā ktālū ʿalā n-nāsi yastawfūn
versets.trad: Those who, when they have themselves served by others, demand full measure,
versets.commentaire: yastawfūn: they demand the full measure. For oneself, one asks everything.

mots.def: iktāla: to receive measure for oneself; reflexive form of kāla.
istawfā: to demand one's full share.

versets.trad: and when they measure or weigh for others, cause them a loss.
versets.commentaire: The reproach widens, as we saw, in Qushayrī's reading. And he gives a splendid definition in contrast: « al-fatā (الفَتَى, the noble youth) is he who renders the rights of people and claims no right of his own from anyone. » The very opposite of the defrauder. Futuwwa (فُتُوَّة), spiritual chivalry, is measured exactly by this gap.

mots.def: kāla: to measure; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: wazana: to weigh; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: akhsara: to cause loss, to damage.

versets.trad: Do these people not think they shall be resurrected
versets.commentaire: A rhetorical question. Qushayrī: “Have they no certainty that they will give account tomorrow, and that they will be asked about the rights of people?” And he adds: “He who does not remember, in dealings with people, the Day of Resurrection and its reckoning, is in loss in his very dealing.”

versets.trad: on a terrible Day,
versets.commentaire: And Qushayrī delivers a precious word: “He who is in vigilance toward the Lord of the worlds feels even now the hayba (هَيْبَة, reverential awe) — as men will at the Gathering — because the gaze of the True today is like His gaze tomorrow.” To live now as though the Resurrection had already happened.

versets.trad: the Day when men shall stand before the Lord of the worlds?
versets.commentaire: yaqūm: they will stand. The qiyāma (Resurrection) is of the same root: it is the great straightening up, where each stands face to face with the Lord of the worlds.

versets.ar: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْفُجَّارِ لَفِي سِجِّينٍ
versets.tr: kallā inna kitāba l-fujjāri la-fī sijjīn
versets.trad: No! Truly, the Book of the libertines is in Sijjīn;
versets.commentaire: Sijjīn (سِجِّين) — Qushayrī: “It is said it is the seventh, lowest earth; there the book of the deeds of the deniers is set down, to humiliate them; then their souls are carried there. It is also said: a pit in Hell. Or: a rock in the lowest earth. Linguistically, it is faʿīl derived from sijn (prison).” The word names then a subterranean prison of the register, where the deeds of the libertines are locked.

mots.def: fujjār: plural of fājir; those who overflow in transgression.
Sijjīn: register of the libertines; also the place where it is kept.

versets.trad: but what will let you know what Sijjīn is?
versets.commentaire: Amplification formula underlining the gravity of the place.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: It is a register kept with care.
versets.commentaire: marqūm: marked, sealed, written precisely. Qushayrī: “God has written in it what they do, and where they are going.” Nothing imprecise in the divine bookkeeping.

mots.def: marqūm: passive participle of raqama; marked with digits or inscriptions.

versets.trad: Woe, that Day, to those who cry lies,
versets.commentaire: The initial wayl is now displaced from the defrauders to the deniers: commercial fraud and metaphysical fraud are of the same register.

mots.def: mukadhdhibīn: plural; those who hold as lies.
mots.gram: preposition + plural active participle, definite genitive

versets.trad: those who hold the Day of Judgment as a lie:
versets.commentaire: The Day of Judgment (yawm al-dīn) — the root d-y-n also means retribution, the accounting of the debt.

versets.ar: وَمَا يُكَذِّبُ بِهِ إِلَّا كُلُّ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ
versets.tr: wa-mā yukadhdhibu bihi illā kullu muʿtadin athīm
versets.trad: only a transgressor, a habitual sinner, holds it as a lie!
versets.commentaire: muʿtad athīm: one who oversteps and one steeped in sin. Qushayrī: “One who passes beyond the limit set for him — when the Quran is recited to him, he disbelieves in it.” The denial of Judgment is rarely the fruit of pure doubt; it is the fruit of a life that has already overstepped the limits and prefers that no account be demanded of it.

mots.def: muʿtad: one who oversteps the limit.
athīm: intensive form; covered in sin.

versets.ar: إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ آيَاتُنَا قَالَ أَسَاطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ
versets.tr: idhā tutlā ʿalayhi āyātunā qāla asāṭīru l-awwalīn
versets.trad: When Our verses are recited to him, he cries: « Fables of the ancients! »
versets.commentaire: asāṭīr al-awwalīn: « writings of the Ancients ». The typical insult of the Meccan deniers: reducing the revelation to old tales. The word asāṭīr names fable, legend, by opposition to serious discourse.

mots.gram: passive imperfect verb, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: asāṭīr: plural of usṭūra; fables, legends.
awwalīn: plural of awwal; the firsts, the ancients.

versets.ar: كَلَّا ۖ بَلْ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم مَّا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ
versets.tr: kallā bal rāna ʿalā qulūbihim mā kānū yaksibūn
versets.trad: No! Their hearts have been hardened by their own deeds.
versets.commentaire: A pivotal verse. rāna (رَانَ): the verb names the rust or patina that settles on a surface to the point of veiling it. Qushayrī: “what they acquired of transgressions has covered their hearts.” The image is precise: sin does not pierce the heart at one blow — it settles in a thin layer, then in superposed layers, until it forms a covering that does not let the light pass. It is the opposite of ṣaqala al-qulūb, “polishing the hearts” through dhikr.

mots.def: rāna: to rust, to cover with a veiling layer.

versets.ar: كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّمَحْجُوبُونَ
versets.tr: kallā innahum ʿan rabbihim yawmaʾidhin la-maḥjūbūn
versets.trad: No! They shall, that Day, be veiled from their Lord by a veil,
versets.commentaire: An overwhelming verse — one of the deepest in the Quran for Sufi thought. maḥjūbūn: veiled, separated by a veil (ḥijāb, حِجَاب). Qushayrī draws from this verse an inference one will not forget: “As they are today prevented from His knowledge, they will be tomorrow prevented from His sight. And the logic of the verse entails that the believers, for their part, will see Him tomorrow as they know Him today.” In this inference all the spirituality of ruʾyat Allāh — the vision of God — finds its ground. And it also follows that the worst of pains, for those of Sijjīn, is not the furnace — it is the veil. What they refused here (knowledge), they are there deprived of (vision). And what would Paradise be if one did not see God there? What would Hell be if one still saw Him?

mots.def: maḥjūb: passive participle of ḥajaba; separated by a veil. Root of ḥijāb.

versets.trad: then they shall burn in the Furnace.
versets.commentaire: The Furnace comes after the veil. The separation from God is first; physical suffering is its consequence.

versets.ar: ثُمَّ يُقَالُ هَٰذَا الَّذِي كُنتُم بِهِ تُكَذِّبُونَ
versets.tr: thumma yuqālu hādhā lladhī kuntum bihi tukadhdhibūn
versets.trad: Then it will be said to them: « This is what you used to deny! »
versets.commentaire: The final acknowledgment is a simple recognition: here is what you denied. No further argument; reality speaks for itself.

mots.gram: passive imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.ar: كَلَّا إِنَّ كِتَابَ الْأَبْرَارِ لَفِي عِلِّيِّينَ
versets.tr: kallā inna kitāba l-abrāri la-fī ʿilliyyīn
versets.trad: No! Truly, the Book of the just is in ʿIlliyyīn;
versets.commentaire: ʿIlliyyīn (عِلِّيِّين) — Qushayrī: “the highest of places, to which the souls of the just are borne for their honour and dignity. It is also said: the Lotus of the Limit (Sidrat al-muntahā). It is said: above the seventh heaven.” The root ʿ-l-w is that of height — it is the inverse image of Sijjīn (the subterranean prison): an elevated palace where the deeds of the abrār (the just, the pious) are kept.

mots.def: abrār: plural of barr; the pious, the benevolent.
ʿIlliyyīn: proper name of the register of the just; literally « the Heights ».

versets.trad: but what will let you know what ʿIlliyyīn is?
versets.commentaire: The mystery here is the inverse: no longer the depth of the prison, but the height of nobility. And the same oratory formula says, on both sides, that one cannot know in advance — one must be there to understand.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: It is a register kept with care.
versets.commentaire: Exact echo of verse 9: same formulation for Sijjīn and for ʿIlliyyīn — a same care in the keeping of the two registers. God loses nothing, on neither side.

versets.trad: Those who are close to God contemplate it.
versets.commentaire: al-muqarrabūn (المُقَرَّبُون) — Qushayrī: “the rapprochés among the angels, witnesses of the register.” In Sufi thought the muqarrabūn are the highest category: above the just (abrār), there are the closest (muqarrabūn) — those who, already in this world, are occupied with no one but Him.

mots.def: shahida: to witness, to contemplate; with suffix pronoun.
mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: muqarrabūn: plural of muqarrab; the rapprochés (of God).

versets.trad: Truly, the just are set in a place of delight;
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī gives a luminous reading, in two times: « today and tomorrow. Today in the breath of gnosis, in the rest of obedience and benevolence, in the grace of contentment, in the intimacy of nearness, and in the expansion of joining. And tomorrow in the Garden, and in all the varieties of nearness and intimacy promised to them. » The naʿīm of the just begins now.

versets.trad: stretched upon couches of state, they gaze...
versets.commentaire: A delicate verse. The Quran says “they gaze” without specifying what they gaze at. Qushayrī seizes upon this: “He set the gaze without determining its object, because of the diversity of their states. Some gaze at their palaces. Some gaze at their houris. Some gaze... and some. And there are the khawāṣṣ: they gaze upon God — glory be to Him — continually.” Three levels of gazing, from palaces to houris to the vision of God Himself.

mots.def: arāʾik: plural of arīka; couches of state, divans.

versets.trad: You recognise on their faces the radiance of beatitude.
versets.commentaire: Qushayrī takes flight here in one of the most beautiful passages of his whole commentary: “He who looks at them knows that the trace of their gaze toward their Master is what appears on their face. The states of the lover are always witnesses upon him. If it is the time of union: his strutting and his coquetry, his joy and his rapture, his energy and his expansion. If it is the time of absence and separation: his pallor and his wasting, his groaning and his sobbing, his tears and his sleeplessness.” The lover's face is the public tableau of his intimacy. And Qushayrī cites a verse of his own composition: « O you whose sight has changed my features, giving reason to all that was suspected of us… »

versets.trad: They will be given a sealed nectar to drink,
versets.commentaire: raḥīq makhtūm: a sealed nectar. Qushayrī gives four readings: “a nectar without mixture; old and excellent; that they will drink down to a residue of musk; or else: sealed before their arrival, set aside for each by name.” This last reading is splendid: what is drunk in Paradise has been prepared for oneself, sealed in expectation of the guest.

mots.sens: they will be given to drink
mots.gram: passive imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

mots.def: makhtūm: passive participle of khatama; sealed.

versets.ar: خِتَامُهُ مِسْكٌ ۚ وَفِي ذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَتَنَافَسِ الْمُتَنَافِسُونَ
versets.tr: khitāmuhu miskun wa-fī dhālika fa-l-yatanāfasi l-mutanāfisūn
versets.trad: with a seal of musk. May those who desire it strive to obtain it!
versets.commentaire: fa-l-yatanāfas: may they vie. Qushayrī lists the ways of this noble rivalry (munāfasa, مُنَافَسَة): “through eagerness for righteous deeds, racing to nearness, attaching the heart to God, stripping off base qualities, the movement of aspirations in the Kingdom, the continuation of intimate conversation.” Six ways for a single race.

mots.gram: energetic imperative verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

versets.trad: It is mixed with the water of Tasnīm,
versets.commentaire: Tasnīm (تَسْنِيم) — Qushayrī: “a spring that rises upon them from above. It is also said: a spout that pours down upon them from above them. It is called Tasnīm because its water flows through the air from height to height, then pours into the cups of the people of Paradise.” The root s-n-m is that of height, of elevation.

mots.def: Tasnīm: spring of elevation in the Garden.

versets.trad: a fountain from which those who are close to God drink.
versets.commentaire: And Qushayrī delivers the perfect spiritual hierarchy: “Among people, some drink it mixed, some drink it pure. The awliyāʾ (friends of God) drink mixed; the khawāṣṣ (the elite) drink pure.” Three degrees: the just (abrār) drink the raḥīq mixed with a little Tasnīm; the friends (awliyāʾ) drink more Tasnīm; the rapprochés (muqarrabūn) drink the source itself, unmixed. The nearer one draws, the purer the drink becomes.

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا كَانُوا مِنَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا يَضْحَكُونَ
versets.tr: inna lladhīna ajramū kānū mina lladhīna āmanū yaḍḥakūn
versets.trad: Truly, the criminals used to laugh at those who believe,
versets.commentaire: The verse pivots to what was happening down here: the guilty laughed at the believers. Qushayrī: “They laughed in mockery.” The sūra is about to reverse the relationship.

mots.def: ajrama: to commit a crime, to transgress.

versets.trad: when they passed by them, they exchanged winks,
versets.commentaire: A chilling detail. yataghāmazūn: they made mocking winks at one another. Contempt is not only loud — it has its secret language, signs among insiders against those they disdain.

mots.def: taghāmaza: to make mocking signs mutually.

versets.ar: وَإِذَا انقَلَبُوا إِلَىٰ أَهْلِهِمُ انقَلَبُوا فَكِهِينَ
versets.tr: wa-idhā nqalabū ilā ahlihimu nqalabū fakihīn
versets.trad: and they returned to their family in jest.
versets.commentaire: fakihīn: amused, hilarious. The mockery becomes a spectacle one brings home, like a good memory.

versets.ar: وَإِذَا رَأَوْهُمْ قَالُوا إِنَّ هَٰؤُلَاءِ لَضَالُّونَ
versets.tr: wa-idhā raʾawhum qālū inna hāʾulāʾi la-ḍāllūn
versets.trad: They would say upon seeing them: « These are surely men gone astray! »
versets.commentaire: The point of the reversal: those who are on the way pass for astray in the eyes of those who go astray. It is the very experience of every wayfarer: his path looks, from the outside, like a wandering.

mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

versets.trad: Yet they were not sent to be their keepers.
versets.commentaire: An implicit retort: they arrogated to themselves a role of judges and keepers that no mission had given them. No one had appointed them. All criticism not mandated by responsibility for the other is but arrogance.

mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. plur.

versets.ar: فَالْيَوْمَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مِنَ الْكُفَّارِ يَضْحَكُونَ
versets.tr: fa-l-yawma lladhīna āmanū mina l-kuffāri yaḍḥakūn
versets.trad: But today it is the believers who laugh at the disbelievers:
versets.commentaire: The laughter is reversed. But Qushayrī discreetly specifies the character of the laughter of the just: it is not scornful; it is the acknowledgment of truth now made manifest.

versets.trad: stretched upon couches of state, they gaze...
versets.commentaire: Textual repetition of verse 23: “upon the couches of state, they gaze.” And one hears, in the repetition, the echo of Qushayrī's reading: the khawāṣṣ gaze upon God continually. The true gaze, in the end, is that one.

versets.ar: هَلْ ثُوِّبَ الْكُفَّارُ مَا كَانُوا يَفْعَلُونَ
versets.tr: hal thuwwiba l-kuffāru mā kānū yafʿalūn
versets.trad: Have the disbelievers been requited for what they used to do?
versets.commentaire: A rhetorical question of closure. Qushayrī: “interrogation with affirmative value.” Yes, they have been requited. The sūra, opened by woe to the defrauders, closes on equity accomplished: each has received the just measure of his deeds — and this very equity is, for the just, an additional joy.

mots.def: thawwaba in the passive; to requite.
mots.gram: passive perfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.
